---
title: "CodeTrain Milestone Assessment Rubrics"
subtitle: "Grading criteria for Demo Days 1–4"
author: "Speedykom Group GmbH"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    papersize: letter
    geometry: margin=1in
    number-sections: true
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: false
---

## Using These Rubrics

Each Demo Day has a **25-point rubric**. Scores:

- **23–25:** Excellent (A) 
- **20–22:** Very Good (B) 
- **18–19:** Good (C) 
- **15–17:** Pass (D) 
- **<15:** Needs Improvement

Students must score **18+ to pass the milestone** and proceed to the next phase.

---

## Mini Demo Day 1: Python Logic Script (Week 4)

**Goal:** Students demonstrate Python mastery through a working CLI tool.

**Submission Format:**

- Working Python script (`.py` file)
- README.md with usage instructions
- GitHub repository link
- 10-minute live demo + 5-minute Q&A

**Example Projects:**

- MTN subscription manager (add/remove users, check balance)
- Voting system for school elections
- Simple expense tracker for Accra small businesses
- Student grade calculator

### Scoring Rubric (25 points)

| Criterion | Points | Excellent (5) | Good (3-4) | Needs Work (1-2) |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Code Quality** | 5 | Clean, Pythonic, good naming | Mostly clean, minor issues | Hard to read, non-Pythonic |
| **Functionality** | 10 | All features work perfectly, no bugs | Most features work | Many bugs, incomplete |
| **Error Handling** | 5 | Robust try/except for edge cases | Some error handling | No error handling |
| **Documentation** | 3 | Clear README, docstrings, comments | Partial docs | Minimal/no docs |
| **Presentation** | 2 | Clear demo, answers Q&A well | Adequate demo | Unclear or unprepared |

**Minimum Score to Pass:** 18/25

---

## Mini Demo Day 2: Exploratory Data Analysis Report (Week 8)

**Goal:** Students demonstrate Pandas mastery and data storytelling.

**Submission Format:**

- Jupyter notebook (`.ipynb`)
- PDF report (1–2 pages with key findings + 3 visualizations)
- 5-slide summary presentation
- GitHub repository link
- 10-minute live demo + 5-minute Q&A

**Required Analysis:**

- Load and explore dataset (10+ data questions answered)
- 5+ publication-ready visualizations
- Identify and fix 3+ data quality issues
- Write 1-page executive summary with key insights

**Example Datasets:**

- Ghana student performance data (district, cohort, scores)
- MTN mobile money transactions (volume, growth, fraud patterns)
- Accra housing market (price trends, district comparisons)
- Ghana health clinic data (patient volume, disease prevalence)

### Scoring Rubric (25 points)

| Criterion | Points | Excellent (5) | Good (3-4) | Needs Work (1-2) |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Data Exploration** | 5 | Thorough; answers 10+ meaningful questions | Adequate; 5-7 questions | Shallow; <5 questions |
| **Visualizations** | 8 | 5+ clear, labeled, insightful plots | 3-4 adequate plots | <3 plots or poor quality |
| **Data Cleaning** | 5 | Handles missing, duplicates, outliers well | Handles some issues | Minimal cleaning |
| **Insights & Storytelling** | 4 | Clear narrative, actionable findings | Basic findings | Vague or unclear |
| **Presentation** | 3 | Professional slides, clear demo | Adequate presentation | Disorganized or unclear |

**Minimum Score to Pass:** 18/25

---

## Mini Demo Day 3: Predictive Model Build (Week 12)

**Goal:** Students demonstrate ML competency: train, evaluate, and interpret models.

**Submission Format:**

- Saved trained model (`.pkl` or `.joblib`)
- Jupyter notebook with full pipeline
- Evaluation report (2–3 pages) with metrics and interpretation
- GitHub repository link
- 12-minute live demo + 5-minute Q&A

**Required Deliverables:**

1. **Data Preprocessing:** Feature engineering, train/test split
2. **Model Training:** At least 3 models (e.g., logistic regression, tree, Random Forest)
3. **Evaluation:** Confusion matrix, precision/recall/F1, ROC curve
4. **Interpretation:** Feature importance, explain top 3 predictions
5. **Conclusion:** Model limitations, real-world applicability

**Example Projects:**

- Ghana student dropout prediction (identify at-risk students early)
- Credit scoring model for MTN Mobile Money
- Cassava disease classification (healthy vs diseased)
- Accra housing price prediction (regression)

### Scoring Rubric (25 points)

| Criterion | Points | Excellent (5) | Good (3-4) | Needs Work (1-2) |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Data Pipeline** | 5 | Clean preprocessing; no leakage | Mostly clean; minor issues | Data leakage or poor splits |
| **Model Variety** | 5 | 3+ models; thoughtful selection | 2-3 models; adequate | 1 model or poor choices |
| **Evaluation** | 8 | Complete metrics; ROC/CAM plots | Basic metrics; missing plots | Incomplete evaluation |
| **Interpretation** | 4 | Clear feature importance; explains predictions | Basic interpretation | Limited interpretation |
| **Presentation** | 3 | Professional report, clear demo | Adequate presentation | Disorganized or unclear |

**Minimum Score to Pass:** 18/25

---

## Final Demo Day: Capstone AI Solution (Week 16)

**Goal:** Students deliver a portfolio-ready AI project demonstrating all 4 phases.

**Team Size:** 2–3 students per team

**Submission Format:**

- GitHub repository (code, documentation, README)
- Trained model(s) / deployed agent
- Technical report (3–5 pages)
- 10-slide presentation deck
- Demo video (optional but encouraged)
- 15-minute live presentation + 10-minute Q&A
- Team roles document (who did what)

**Required Deliverables:**

1. **Problem Framing:** Real Ghana use case; why it matters
2. **Data:** Real or realistic dataset; describe source
3. **Pipeline:** End-to-end: load → clean → engineer → train → evaluate → deploy
4. **Model:** Working model or agent
5. **Evaluation:** Metrics, visualizations, test results
6. **Interpretation:** What does the model do well? Where does it fail?
7. **Deployment:** Can someone else run this code and make predictions?
8. **Documentation:** README, architecture diagram, deployment guide

**Example Projects:**

- **Image Classification:** Cassava disease detection (help Ghanaian farmers diagnose crop disease)
- **NLP Agent:** Customer service chatbot for MTN or mPharma
- **Predictive Model:** Student performance forecasting (intervene early)
- **Time Series:** Electricity demand forecasting for Accra
- **Recommendation System:** Educational content recommendation for students

### Scoring Rubric (50 points)

| Criterion | Points | Excellent (9-10) | Good (7-8) | Pass (5-6) | Needs Work (1-4) |
|-----------|--------|------------------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Problem Framing** | 10 | Clear Ghana context; real impact | Good problem; minor gaps | Adequate problem | Unclear or generic |
| **Technical Depth** | 15 | Strong feature engineering; advanced techniques | Solid ML/DL; good choices | Basic pipeline; simple models | Incomplete or shallow |
| **Evaluation & Metrics** | 10 | Comprehensive; multiple metrics; visualizations | Good metrics; charts | Basic metrics | Minimal evaluation |
| **Code Quality & Docs** | 10 | Professional; reproducible; well-commented | Good structure; docs present | Adequate code; some docs | Poor structure; no docs |
| **Presentation & Delivery** | 5 | Polished deck; clear demo; professional | Good presentation; clear | Adequate; some confusion | Disorganized or unclear |

**Minimum Score to Pass:** 30/50 (60%)

### Presentation Checklist

**15-minute presentation should cover:**

- [ ] Problem: What problem are you solving? Why does it matter in Ghana?
- [ ] Data: Where did you get the data? How much? What challenges?
- [ ] Model: What algorithm? Why this one? Architecture/hyperparameters?
- [ ] Results: What are your metrics? How does it perform?
- [ ] Interpretation: What did you learn? What are the limitations?
- [ ] Demo: Show the model in action on a real example
- [ ] Future: What would you improve next?

**Q&A Tips:**

- "Why did you choose Random Forest over XGBoost?" → Be ready to explain trade-offs
- "How would you handle this failure case?" → Show you thought about limitations
- "Can you deploy this to production?" → Explain your architecture for serving

---

## Assessment Logistics

### Before Demo Day
- [ ] Students submit code and report 48 hours before
- [ ] Instructors review for basic completeness
- [ ] Flag any issues (missing files, broken code) for students to fix

### During Demo Day
- [ ] Allocate 15 min per student (or 20 min per team)
- [ ] Use rubric to score in real-time
- [ ] Take notes on what went well and gaps to improve
- [ ] Share rubric with students after presenting

### After Demo Day
- [ ] Average scores across all students
- [ ] Identify common struggles (feature engineering, model selection, etc.)
- [ ] Adjust next cohort's curriculum based on findings
- [ ] Share top projects as examples for future students

---

## Grade Distribution

For each cohort, expect roughly:

- **18%** Excellent (23–25)
- **35%** Very Good (20–22)
- **32%** Good (18–19)
- **10%** Needs Improvement (<18)

If >15% score below 18, review the phase difficulty and adjust supports.

---

## Bonus: Extra Credit Opportunities

Students can improve their scores by:

- **Mini Demo Days:** Deploy model to web API (Flask/FastAPI)
- **Capstone:** Open-source contribution; submit to Kaggle competition
- **Presentations:** Translate docs to local languages (Twi, Ga)
- **Mentoring:** Help struggling peers with exercises

---

*Rubrics designed for CodeTrain's 16-week curriculum. Adapt based on cohort level.*
